sentences.text: In the ghetto?
In the ghetto, was a small [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gentile-- Gentile people lived there before in that house.
And they had to move out because there was planned for the ghetto, so the Gentile people didn't have a big saying against the Germans either.
It was an order, that that section has to be cleared out by all Gentile people.
And by luck, we got that small-- small, little house.
And we were a family, at that time six people.
So we got that little house, which was not far from our original house, where we lived.
And that was all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told by the Jewish committee, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where we have to go.
Each Jewish family was given orders by the Jewish committee with a number where they are going to stay and where they're going-- going to live.
Because otherwise, it would be a chaos if everybody would run, just grab houses.
Everybody got a certain little letter, an order.
You are going to live right near [LITHUANIAN] street.
Or near [LITHUANIAN] street.
Yes.
They gave you a specific place with the address where you got to go and live.
Yeah.
But near house, near our house was the other little houses, and it was other families.
And then was bigger houses was occupied by two, three families.
And if the house was a lot bigger, was four and five families.
But that was particularly-- we were lucky, it was a very small house.
And it was only like for five, six people to live.
No.
It was like old-fashioned iron little stove.
You had to throw in something to cook.
But at that time, it was not winter yet anyhow.
It was August.
So it was not too cold.
But in order to cook, my mother used to take a couple pieces of wood, make a little fire at the little iron grill.
Excuse me.
And she used to cook.
We didn't have facilities in the ghetto, you know, like electric ovens or something like real modern equipment.
All Slabodke was not modern equipped.
Yes.
Was electricity.
Yeah.
Then it opened up like a couple of weeks later, after the ghetto was established, opened up a-- a little hospital.
And Dr.
Kaplan, Mrs.
Kaplan, and a lot more doctors were taking care on sick people.
Didn't have too much of medicine because the Germans already, you know, start to cut down on medications.
But all the doctors, when they left their homes, or their hospitals where they worked before the war, they went and they got big amount of medicines to bring in in the ghetto.
So it was a little stock of medicine.
Maybe it lasted a year, or-- or three quarter of a year.

Then it start to get short on medicine, and they had to beg the committee to beg the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gestapo to bring at least, you know, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] important medicines against epidemics, against different typhuses.
Or aspirins was an important medicine.
And the Germans at that time, you know, need-- needed some people still to work for them too.
So they gave small amounts of medicine.
But if a man was real terrible sick, the chance was not too good.
In the ghetto, no.
It was no epidemics.
The ghetto, the later years people start to die from hunger.
But as I recall, that-- as I remember, it was not an epidemic in the ghetto.
Yes.
If they didn't die from bad illnesses or hunger.
Or the main thing, of course, selections.
That was the main problem.
You didn't know any morning or any night when the trucks are going to drive in with the green uniforms, with the rifles, with the machine guns.
[GERMAN], [GERMAN].
Everybody out.
So you went to sleep with that fear, and you got up with that fear.
No.
It was no comfort.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were thinking that that's is Hitler's program, to liquidate all the Jews, as many as he can.
First in Europe, and then in the other parts of the world, even the United States.
Because I heard Germans-- as I used to do certain things for them when I worked in the [GERMAN]-- we used to sometime making terrible jokes with me.
He says, don't be worried, Moses.
They are going to have other [GERMAN] in the future, more.
They are going to have even American [GERMAN].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not only Lithuanian [GERMAN].
We are going to have American [GERMAN].
So I knew what he meant.
He meant-- that means they are going to take the whole world.
In 1941, the German country and the German soldiers, that they really believed the German is going to take over the whole world.
I mean, the power and all their taking country after country, the Blitzkrieg with all the weapons, and all the power, that nobody will be able to stop him.
No.
It was no resistance.

Maybe the last half a year [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or maybe the last year or the ghetto start to be organized, the Zionist organization, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] organization start to organize themselves quietly and have meetings how to sabotage the German army, how to do damage towards the German soldiers.
But it was not with big results.
But then start to get big contacts, these organizations in the ghetto, with the outside of the ghetto.
That was the last years of the war-- of the war.
In the woods, in the forests, were a lot of partisans, Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Regular army Russian partisans.
They were with parachutes, you know, down in the forests in Lithuania.
And they got contact, the Jewish organizations in the ghetto.
Three, four organizations with the partisans outside the ghetto, communications.
And then slowly, little by little, a lot of Jews start to sneak out from the ghetto.
And they had already contacts.
And they went in in the woods, and they were working with the Russian, with the Soviet partisans, against the Nazis.
I had a friend of mine, he was maybe a couple of years older than me.
His name was Slapok.
Two brothers.
Slapok.
Very strong athletic boys.
And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got in contact with certain, you know, partisans.
And the partisans gave these two boys orders to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take railroads, dynamite the railroad, rails.
And these two boys, my friends, the two brother Slapoks, killed, I wouldn't exaggerate, thousands of German soldiers who used to come by train, you know, transport soldiers.
The two boys were dressed like peasant boys, Lithuanians, like farmer boys with shovels wearing like they work on farms.
And they were experts [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trained by the Russian partisans in the woods how to dynamite the trains.
And they took a lot of trains off the trains-- off the rails.
And they are still living.
They are in Israel, the two boys.
One name was Rufke.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Rufke and Benjamin.
Ben, Ben.
Rufke and Ben.
Two brother.
After the war, they went to Israel.
And what they did in the war against the Nazis, when the Israelis had problems with the Palestinians, they were a big help there too.
That's right.
But they, these boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dynamite.
A lot of soldiers were flying in the air.
And these boys used to grab live Germans, soldiers, and they used to hang them, and with a sign.
This is for the Jewish blood.
And hide themselves back in the woods.
Because I-- I was with these two boys after the war in the same place.
After the war when we were liberated, you understand.
And I was in Germany.
And I was at-- in that kibbutz, in that [INAUDIBLE], that [GERMAN].
These two boys were sent in to live in that-- in that mansion from that big Nazi.
Because they were-- this was their place, a preparation to make way for Israel.
And there where I met the two boys, they showed me all kinds of things, and medals and everything.
I wanted to join, but I think I didn't want to leave my mother.
I want-- I could-- I didn't have a heart.
If I-- my father would be alive, I would have joined it.
But I didn't have no father, and my mother and the family didn't have that-- that heart to leave her alone.
No.
The only--
The only good thing-- excuse me.
The only good time was if you got a extra loaf of bread by mistake.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or you got a piece of butter in trading with a Gentile person who gave you, you know, like a kilo of butter for a fancy golden ring.
So you were lucky.
Because a lot of times, you traded with them and you were not lucky because it had to be a fast trade.
Guards were all the time watching you.
So it had to be traded fast outside the ghetto.
And one time, my mother gave a woman-- she was walking my mother outside of the ghetto in a factory where they were slaughtering chickens for the German army.
And an old lady-- which I hate to say, with a big cross too-- was trading with my mother.
And my mother gave her a ring, and it was in the wintertime, very cold.
And the woman gave her in a paper, wrapped up, a big piece of butter, supposed to be.
And Mother was happy.
Wow, she got for that little ring a nice big piece of butter.
But when she came home and we opened up the paper and we cut it in was a big stone inside.
The butter was only smeared around the stone.
And the German woman got away with the ring, and we got just a thin covering on a stone with butter.
That was done by a Gentile Lithuanian old lady with a cross.
Yes.
My mother kept her wedding ring.
She would never give out that.
Two things in the crematorium she had to give up is my little brother, and the ring was pulled off before she went to the crematorium.
And she kept it until then.
Your mother--
Actually, she didn't give up my little brother.
She went with him on the lap.
But I mean, she--
In secretly, everything.
No.
The Jews had to wear that Jewish star.
Jewish people who walked-- who were in the ghetto, they used to go out-- out of the ghetto, you were all the time had to wear that star of David.
That was the order in the ghetto when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
It was before, before the ghetto.
It was August-- it was July the 12th.
July the 12th was given that order with every Jew must wear a star.
If they are caught without a star, they are going to be shot immediately.
Everybody had to make stars.
Material.
You give-- you went to the committee if you didn't have, or you went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to friends, and you to find yellow material.
One got from the other, you made stars.
Yes.
It was a certain size, the star.
I think it was like 3 and 1/2 inches.
I don't know on the centimeter now.
But 3 and 1/2 inches.
Almost like 4 inch stars.
On the left side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right, actually, on your heart.

So when people walk-- walked out, you know, as you were walking outside of the ghetto, you were still wearing the star, unless you took a terrible chance and you took off the star so you could go in-- in a lot more areas, because the Germans then didn't know if you are Jewish or not.
But you took a big chance.
If another Lithuanian would notice that you are Jewish and you are not wearing the star, she would right away give you out.
She would call the Germans, say, hey!
Here is a Jew there without a star.
And you were taken and you were shot.
No, they were not rewarded.
I wouldn't say all Lithuanians did it.
But comparing with other countries, they were expert of killing Jews.
Lithuania is known for not killing only the Lithuanish Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They begged the Germans gestapo-- and the gestapo to bring Jews from other countries and there have the pleasure to kill them.
And that's what it was.
I've seen transport from Germany and from other countries, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the trucks, taken to the ninth ward and they were killed.
And the Lithuanians had the biggest pleasures.
The Germans were smoking the cigars and cigarettes, and the Lithuanians with the machine guns proudly finished up only not the Lithuanian Jews, but German Jews, and I think Hungarian Jews too.
And I think French Jews too.
But German Jews I know for sure, because some of the clothing too fell off from the trucks, and Lithuanians find in the pockets, you know?
Clothing.
And some of the clothing from the German Jews they'll send in in the ghetto, in magazines, to give out clothing for the Jews in the ghetto.
And some Jews in the ghetto, Mr.
[LITHUANIAN], who was the chief, you know, in the clothing department, and in the-- in the department of the [GERMAN], was Mr.
[LITHUANIAN].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would-- he hesitated to release from that magazine the German clothes to the Lithuanian Jews, because it-- he didn't want, to, you know, the tragedy should become bigger.
So it was kept in locked magazines in the ghetto.
That's what the Lithuanians did.
In the ghetto, we were from August 15 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until July the 13th, 1944.
From 1941, August the 15th, till-- until July the 13th.
In 1944, they start to liquidate.
The Soviet army start to get closer and closer, chasing.
The Germans start to lose the war.
And they were-- start to retreat from all-- from all over.
From Russia.
Since Stalingrad, you know, they start to go all the way down.
The last couple of years, we knew it.
We got information, you know, from some Lithuanians who had, you know, the special radios.
And some Jewish people work for Lithuanians who were Lithuanians and Germans.
They were like Volksdeutsch.
They lived in Lithuania, but-- but they were Germans.
And they used to call themself Volksdeutsch.
And one German, a Volksdeutsch, his name was Genske.
And he had a brigade of Jewish worker working for him in his machine shop outside of the ghetto, because he was working for the German army.
And he was wearing the brown, you know, band with the swastika, and he had a license to take out like 20 Jews to work for his shop.
He was a Volksdeutsch.
A matter of fact, my father knew him very well.
That Genske.
And that Genske gave all the time the news secretly to a couple of the men who worked for him.
And Genske knew how the war goes, where the losses are, where the second front is, and America entered the-- the war.
Because we really didn't know the big war between the United States and Japan.
We really didn't know too much about that.
We didn't know-- we knew when-- when the-- when the United States enter the war direct with Germany, but not too much.
We didn't know.
Because when we were in the barracks in the-- in Dachau, we used to cry and say, where are the Americans?
Are they sleeping?
What's happening?
Because people are skeletons.
We were dying like flies.
Where are the Americans?
We didn't know exactly that Americans are falling like flies themselves in the war.
Thousands of American good men with airplanes are being shot down, killed, murdered.
I lost my cousins in Italy, you know, in Anzio.
Full death, you know.
Got killed in the war.
We didn't know about those things.
We were just crying in the-- in Dachau.
Where are they?
Why doesn't they come to help us?
So in the ghetto, you were beginning to see the turn in the war that the--
Yeah, we saw that.
After Stalingrad, the Germans became faster to liquidate Jewish population all over in Europe.
And they began the notification--
Because they start to feel already that has got to be sped up faster.
It's got to go with a big tempo, because they saw after Stalingrad that Himmler-- especially him-- he start to feel and to pledge, you know, the pledge he had in his heart to rain wash.
To clean all the Jews, as much as he can.
And it start to work very fast, to liquidate the Jews in the ghettos.
